questTitle: P4 Math Revision: Units, Equal Concept & Patterns
subject: Mathematics
description: Master word problems using units and equal concepts! This worksheet covers comparison problems, equal sharing problems, working backwards, and number patterns. Draw models to help you solve! Read each question carefully.

questions.question: Lisa has 5 times as many sweets as Amy. If we represent Amy's sweets as 1 unit, how many units represents Lisa's sweets?
Lisa has 5 units and Amy has 1 unit. How many units do they have altogether?
Lisa has 5 units and Amy has 1 unit. Together they have 6 units which equals 48 sweets. What is the value of 1 unit?

questions.problemSet: PROBLEM SET 1: Comparison - Before and After
questions.question: Lisa has 5 times as many sweets as Amy. If Amy eats 10 sweets and Lisa eats 6 sweets, who eats more sweets?

questions.problemSet: PROBLEM SET 1: Comparison - Before and After (continued)
questions.question: After eating, Lisa has 6 times as many sweets as Amy. If Amy has 1 part now, how many parts does Lisa have?

questions.problemSet: PROBLEM SET 1: Comparison - Before and After (continued)
questions.question: At first, the difference between them was 4 units. After eating, the difference became 5 parts. Amy ate 10 sweets and Lisa ate 6 sweets. How much did the difference between them increase by?

questions.problemSet: PROBLEM SET 1: Comparison - Before and After ⭐ MAIN QUESTION
questions.question: Lisa has 5 times as many sweets as Amy. After Lisa ate 6 sweets and Amy ate 10 sweets, Lisa now has 6 times as many sweets as Amy. How many sweets did Amy have at first?
Ken and Danny have an equal number of cards at first. If Ken has 1 unit, how many units does Danny have?
Ken and Danny each have 1 unit at first. Ken then GIVES Danny 54 cards. What happens to the difference between their amounts?
After the transfer, Danny has 4 times as many cards as Ken. If Ken now has 1 part, how many parts do they have altogether?
The difference between them is now 3 parts. We know the difference increased by 108 cards due to the transfer. What is the value of 1 part?

questions.problemSet: PROBLEM SET 2: Equal at First ⭐ MAIN QUESTION
questions.question: Ken and Danny have an equal number of cards at first. After Ken gave Danny 54 cards, Danny has 4 times as many cards as Ken. How many cards did they have altogether at first?

questions.problemSet: PROBLEM SET 3: More Than - Before and After
questions.question: Sarah has 24 more stickers than Mei. If Mei has 1 unit, how can we represent Sarah's stickers?

questions.problemSet: PROBLEM SET 3: More Than - Before and After (continued)
questions.question: Sarah gives Mei some stickers. After that, Mei has 3 times as many as Sarah. If Sarah now has 1 part, how many parts do they have altogether?

questions.problemSet: PROBLEM SET 3: More Than - Before and After (continued)
questions.question: Mei had 42 stickers at first. Sarah had 24 more than Mei. What was the total number of stickers they had at first?

questions.problemSet: PROBLEM SET 3: More Than - Before and After ⭐ MAIN QUESTION
questions.question: Sarah has 24 more stickers than Mei. After Sarah gives some stickers to Mei, Mei has 3 times as many stickers as Sarah. If Mei had 42 stickers at first, how many stickers did Sarah give to Mei?
In 'working backwards' problems, we start from the END and work back to the BEGINNING. If a number is doubled (×2), what do we do when working backwards?
If 15 was added to a number, what do we do when working backwards?
Tom thinks of a number. He multiplies it by 3, then adds 12. His answer is 45. To find Tom's original number, what is the first step working backwards from 45?
Continuing from the previous question: After subtracting 12 from 45, what is the next step to find Tom's original number?

questions.problemSet: PROBLEM SET 4: Working Backwards ⭐ MAIN QUESTION
questions.question: Jenny thinks of a number. She divides it by 4, then subtracts 8, then multiplies by 5. Her answer is 60. What was Jenny's original number?
Candice eats 2 more sweets each day than the day before. If she eats 5 sweets on Day 1, how many does she eat on Day 2?
Candice eats 2 more sweets each day than the day before. Day 1: 5, Day 2: 7, Day 3: 9. How many sweets does she eat on Day 4?
Candice eats 19 sweets on Day 4. The pattern increases by 2 each day. How many sweets did she eat on Day 2?

questions.problemSet: PROBLEM SET 5: Number Patterns - Increasing ⭐ MAIN QUESTION
questions.question: Candice eats 2 more sweets each day than the day before. If she is going to eat 19 sweets on Day 4, how many sweets did she eat on Day 1?
Ben reads 5 fewer pages each day than the day before. If he reads 40 pages on Day 1, how many pages does he read on Day 2?
Ben reads 5 fewer pages each day. Day 1: 40, Day 2: 35, Day 3: 30, Day 4: 25. On which day does he read 10 pages?
Ben reads 10 pages on Day 7. The pattern decreases by 5 each day. How many pages did he read on Day 1?

questions.problemSet: PROBLEM SET 6: Number Patterns - Decreasing ⭐ MAIN QUESTION
questions.question: Ben reads 5 fewer pages each day than the day before. On Day 5, he reads 22 pages. How many pages did he read on Day 1?

questions.problemSet: PROBLEM SET 7: Times As Many - Transfer
questions.question: Ali has 3 times as many marbles as Ben. If Ben has 1 unit, Ali has 3 units. What is the total number of units?

questions.problemSet: PROBLEM SET 7: Times As Many - Transfer (continued)
questions.question: Ali gives Ben 14 marbles. After this, they have the same number of marbles. How many more marbles did Ali have than Ben at first?

questions.problemSet: PROBLEM SET 7: Times As Many - Transfer (continued)
questions.question: At first, Ali had 2 units more than Ben. We know this difference is 28 marbles. How many marbles is 1 unit equal to?

questions.problemSet: PROBLEM SET 7: Times As Many - Transfer ⭐ MAIN QUESTION
questions.question: Ali has 3 times as many marbles as Ben. After Ali gives Ben 14 marbles, they have the same number of marbles. How many marbles did Ali have at first?

questions.problemSet: PROBLEM SET 8: Equal - Both Give Away
questions.question: Lily and Rose have an equal number of beads at first. Lily gives away 15 beads and Rose gives away 36 beads. Who gives away more beads?

questions.problemSet: PROBLEM SET 8: Equal - Both Give Away (continued)
questions.question: Lily and Rose started with equal amounts. Rose gave 21 more beads than Lily. What is the difference between their remaining amounts?

questions.problemSet: PROBLEM SET 8: Equal - Both Give Away (continued)
questions.question: After giving away beads, Lily has 4 times as many beads as Rose. The difference between them is 21 beads. What is the value of 1 part (Rose's remaining beads)?

questions.problemSet: PROBLEM SET 8: Equal - Both Give Away (NEW QUESTION)
questions.question: Lily and Rose have an equal number of beads at first. Lily gives away 12 beads and Rose gives away 44 beads. After that, Lily has 5 times as many beads as Rose. What is the value of 1 part (Rose's remaining beads)?

questions.problemSet: PROBLEM SET 8: Equal - Both Give Away ⭐ MAIN QUESTION
questions.question: Lily and Rose have an equal number of beads at first. Lily gives away 8 beads and Rose gives away 48 beads. After that, Lily has 3 times as many beads as Rose. How many beads did they have altogether at first?
A bacteria colony doubles every hour. If there are 8 bacteria at Hour 1, how many are there at Hour 2?
The bacteria doubles every hour. If there are 256 bacteria at Hour 6, how many were there at Hour 5?

questions.problemSet: PROBLEM SET 9: Doubling Pattern ⭐ MAIN QUESTION
questions.question: A bacteria colony doubles every hour. At Hour 5, there are 160 bacteria. How many bacteria were there at Hour 1?
Cindy has twice as many stickers as Diana. Together they have 45 stickers. If Diana has 1 unit, what is the value of 1 unit?
Cindy has 30 stickers and Diana has 15 stickers. Cindy buys 12 more stickers. Diana gives away 3 stickers. What is the new ratio of Cindy's stickers to Diana's stickers?

questions.problemSet: PROBLEM SET 10: Combined Units Problem ⭐ MAIN QUESTION
questions.question: Cindy has twice as many stickers as Diana. Cindy then buys 18 more stickers while Diana gives away 6 stickers. Now Cindy has 4 times as many stickers as Diana. How many stickers did Cindy have at first?
On Day 1, Emma saves $3. Each day she saves $4 more than the day before. What does she save on Day 4?
Emma saves: Day 1 = $3, Day 2 = $7, Day 3 = $11, Day 4 = $15. How much does she save in total over the 4 days?

questions.problemSet: PROBLEM SET 11: Pattern with Sum ⭐ MAIN QUESTION
questions.question: Emma saves $5 on Day 1. Each day she saves $3 more than the day before. After how many days will her total savings first exceed $50?
Peter has 4 times as many coins as Queenie. They have 60 coins altogether. How many coins does Peter have?
A number is multiplied by 5, then 18 is subtracted, and the result is divided by 2. The final answer is 16. What was the original number?
Ryan and Sam have an equal number of game cards. Ryan gives Sam 24 cards. Sam then has 3 times as many cards as Ryan. How many cards did Ryan have at first?
